components.name: Accordion
components.description: Accessible accordion built on native <details> / <summary>. Supports single-expand and multi-expand modes, controlled and uncontrolled state, keyboard navigation (ArrowDown/Up/Home/End), focus management into panels on expand, and compile-time enforcement of accessible trigger names.

props.name: headingLevel
props.description: Heading level rendered by every Accordion.Header. Required — no default, so authors must pick a level that fits the document outline.

props.name: type
props.description: Controls whether at most one item can be open ("single") or multiple items can be open simultaneously ("multiple").

props.name: value
props.description: Controlled open value. String for single mode, string[] for multiple. Pair with onValueChange.

props.name: defaultValue
props.description: Uncontrolled initial open value.

props.name: onValueChange
props.description: Called when the open state changes from a user interaction. Required when value is provided.

props.name: children
props.description: One or more Accordion.Item elements. A dev overlay fires when no Items are rendered.

props.name: className
props.description: Additional CSS class applied to the accordion root.

props.name: Item.value
props.description: Stable identifier for this item. Must be unique within the accordion; a dev overlay fires on duplicate values.

props.name: Item.disabled
props.description: Marks the item non-interactive. The summary receives aria-disabled=true and is excluded from Arrow-key navigation.

props.name: Item.children
props.description: Must contain exactly one Accordion.Header (with Accordion.Trigger inside) and one Accordion.Panel.

props.name: Trigger.children
props.description: Visible label text for the trigger. Exactly one of children, aria-label, or aria-labelledby is required — compile error otherwise.

props.name: Trigger.aria-label
props.description: Invisible label for icon-only triggers. Mutually exclusive with children and aria-labelledby.

props.name: Trigger.aria-labelledby
props.description: ID of an external label element. Mutually exclusive with children and aria-label.

props.name: Panel.children
props.description: Content shown when the item is open.

props.name: Panel.className
props.description: Additional CSS class applied to the panel element.

accessibility.description: Built on native <details> / <summary> — the trigger-to-panel relationship is implicit in the DOM and communicated to assistive technology without aria-controls. aria-controls is also set explicitly as a defensive measure for older AT.
The expand/collapse indicator is a custom chevron rendered with aria-hidden=true. The UA disclosure marker is suppressed via CSS so it does not create a duplicate or confusing indicator.

Enter and Space toggle the disclosure natively. ArrowDown/Up/Home/End navigate between summaries without toggling (APG accordion keyboard contract). Disabled items are excluded from Arrow-key navigation.

On a user-initiated expand (click or Enter/Space), focus moves into the panel so screen reader users hear the newly revealed content immediately. Programmatic opens (controlled value changes) do not move focus.
Focus indicators use outline (not box-shadow) to remain visible in Windows High Contrast Mode.
The summary element has a minimum 44px block-size and is full-width, providing a 44×44 CSS px touch target.
Each item's trigger is wrapped in a real heading (h2–h6). headingLevel is required on the root with no default to force authors to choose a level that fits the page outline.

The summary carries aria-expanded (mirrors the open attribute for older AT) and aria-controls. Accessible names on Accordion.Trigger are enforced at compile time via AccessibleNameProps. Dev overlays fire for missing triggers, missing panels, duplicate values, and empty accordions.

examples.name: Basic single-expand
examples.description: Uncontrolled accordion where at most one item is open.
examples.code:
Introduction

Welcome to the FAQ section.

 How do I use this?

Click any section header to expand it.

examples.name: Multi-expand
examples.description: Multiple items can be open simultaneously.
examples.code:
<Accordion type="multiple" headingLevel={3}>
  <Accordion.Item value="a">
    <Accordion.Header>
      <Accordion.Trigger>Feature A</Accordion.Trigger>
    </Accordion.Header>
    <Accordion.Panel>Details about Feature A.</Accordion.Panel>
  </Accordion.Item>
  <Accordion.Item value="b">
    <Accordion.Header>
      <Accordion.Trigger>Feature B</Accordion.Trigger>
    </Accordion.Header>
    <Accordion.Panel>Details about Feature B.</Accordion.Panel>
  </Accordion.Item>
</Accordion>

examples.name: Controlled
examples.description: Open state managed by external state.
examples.code:
const [open, setOpen] = useState('');

<Accordion type="single" value={open} onValueChange={setOpen} headingLevel={3}>
  <Accordion.Item value="a">
    <Accordion.Header>
      <Accordion.Trigger>Section A</Accordion.Trigger>
    </Accordion.Header>
    <Accordion.Panel>Content A</Accordion.Panel>
  </Accordion.Item>
</Accordion>

examples.name: With disabled item
examples.description: A disabled item is excluded from keyboard navigation.
examples.code:
<Accordion headingLevel={3}>
  <Accordion.Item value="a">
    <Accordion.Header>
      <Accordion.Trigger>Active section</Accordion.Trigger>
    </Accordion.Header>
    <Accordion.Panel>Content</Accordion.Panel>
  </Accordion.Item>
  <Accordion.Item value="b" disabled>
    <Accordion.Header>
      <Accordion.Trigger>Locked section</Accordion.Trigger>
    </Accordion.Header>
    <Accordion.Panel>Unavailable content</Accordion.Panel>
  </Accordion.Item>
</Accordion>

donts.reason: Empty string is not an accessible name. Compile error — AccessibleText rejects placeholder values.
Placeholder strings like 'image' are not accessible names. Compile error.
Item without a Trigger. A dev overlay fires with the key Accordion:item-without-trigger.
Accordion with no Items. A dev overlay fires with the key Accordion:empty.

fileContents.content:
/**
 * Compile-time accessibility guarantees.
 *
 * These types make missing or placeholder accessibility data a TypeScript
 * error, not a silent runtime mistake. They are the §1.4.2 "fouten onmogelijk
 * maken via TypeScript" layer of the artui enforcement model.
 */

/**
 * Strings that look like alt text but communicate nothing to a screenreader.
 * Authors using these have not actually written alt text — they have written
 * a placeholder. The Image component rejects these at compile time.
 */
export type PlaceholderAltText =
  | 'image'
  | 'Image'
  | 'IMAGE'
  | 'img'
  | 'Img'
  | 'IMG'
  | 'photo'
  | 'Photo'
  | 'picture'
  | 'Picture'
  | 'icon'
  | 'Icon'
  | 'logo'
  | 'Logo'
  | ''
  | ' ';

/**
 * A non-empty, non-placeholder string. Used wherever an accessible name is
 * required (alt, aria-label, ...). Authors trying to pass `""` or `"image"`
 * get a type error.
 */
export type AccessibleText<T extends string = string> = T extends PlaceholderAltText
  ? never
  : T;

/**
 * Discriminated union for any interactive component that requires an
 * accessible name. Exactly one of these three sources MUST be present:
 *
 *  - visible text content (`children: string`)
 *  - `aria-label`
 *  - `aria-labelledby` (pointing at another element)
 *
 * Used by Button, IconButton, Modal close-button, and similar.
 */
export type AccessibleNameProps =
  | { children: AccessibleText; 'aria-label'?: never; 'aria-labelledby'?: never }
  | { children?: never; 'aria-label': AccessibleText; 'aria-labelledby'?: never }
  | { children?: never; 'aria-label'?: never; 'aria-labelledby': string };

components.name: Datepicker
components.description: Accessible date input with calendar popup. Follows WAI-ARIA APG Date Picker Dialog pattern. Passes all 9 WCAG criteria where peer libraries fail.

props.name: label
props.description: Visible label for the date input. Exactly one of label, aria-label, or aria-labelledby is required — passing none or two is a compile error.

props.name: aria-label
props.description: Non-visible accessible name. Use when a visible label is not desired. Mutually exclusive with label and aria-labelledby.

props.name: aria-labelledby
props.description: ID of an existing element that labels this input. Mutually exclusive with label and aria-label.

props.name: value
props.description: The currently selected date, or null when no date is selected.

props.name: onChange
props.description: Called when the user selects a date via the calendar or types a valid date.

props.name: locale
props.description: BCP-47 locale tag controlling date format and calendar week start. Defaults to the browser language. Passing an invalid BCP-47 tag triggers a dev overlay.

props.name: min
props.description: Earliest selectable date (inclusive). Days before this are disabled.

props.name: max
props.description: Latest selectable date (inclusive). Days after this are disabled.

props.name: isDateDisabled
props.description: Custom predicate to disable individual dates.

props.name: error
props.description: External validation error message displayed below the input and wired to the input via aria-describedby.

props.name: required
props.description: Maps to aria-required on the text input.

accessibility.description: Text input is a first-class entry path. Parse errors are wired to the input via aria-describedby. The calendar heading is a semantic <h2>.
All controls are native <button> elements. Arrow keys navigate the day grid. Tab cycles only among prev/next/close buttons inside the dialog.
Focus trap inside the dialog is limited to three buttons (prev, next, close). Escape always exits. Selecting a date also exits.
DOM order enforces logical focus sequence: prev-month → next-month → close button. Dialog is role="dialog" with aria-labelledby pointing at the month/year <h2>.
Focus ring uses outline (not box-shadow) on every interactive element including day cells, preserving visibility in Windows High Contrast mode.
Invalid typed dates produce an error message that is referenced by the input's aria-describedby. External errors from the error prop are handled identically.
The label prop renders a visible <label> element. aria-label and aria-labelledby are alternate sources. Exactly one is required at compile time.
Selected day has aria-selected="true". Today has aria-current="date". Nav buttons have aria-label with the target month name. aria-haspopup="dialog" and aria-expanded on the trigger.
An aria-live="polite" aria-atomic region always present in the DOM announces the new month name when the user navigates between months.

examples.name: Controlled with visible label
examples.description: Standard controlled usage with a visible label.
examples.code:
const [date, setDate] = useState<Date | null>(null);
<Datepicker label="Appointment date" value={date} onChange={setDate} />

examples.name: With min/max range
examples.description: Restricts selectable dates to the current year.
examples.code:
<Datepicker
  label="Event date"
  value={date}
  onChange={setDate}
  min={new Date(2026, 0, 1)}
  max={new Date(2026, 11, 31)}
/>

examples.name: With isDateDisabled
examples.description: Blocks weekends from being selectable.
examples.code:
<Datepicker
  label="Delivery date"
  value={date}
  onChange={setDate}
  isDateDisabled={(d) => d.getDay() === 0 || d.getDay() === 6}
/>

examples.name: With external error
examples.description: Error from server-side validation wired to the input.
examples.code:
<Datepicker
  label="Birth date"
  value={date}
  onChange={setDate}
  error={serverError}
/>

examples.name: nl-BE locale
examples.description: Belgian Dutch locale with DD/MM/YYYY format and Monday week start.
examples.code: <Datepicker label="Datum" value={date} onChange={setDate} locale="nl-BE" />

donts.reason: Missing label source. Compile error — every Datepicker must have label, aria-label, or aria-labelledby so screen reader users know what the field is for.
Two label sources at once. Compile error — pass exactly one of label, aria-label, or aria-labelledby.
Invalid BCP-47 locale tag — dev overlay fires and the component falls back to navigator.language.

fileContents.content:
/**
 * Compile-time accessibility guarantees.
 *
 * These types make missing or placeholder accessibility data a TypeScript
 * error, not a silent runtime mistake. They are the §1.4.2 "fouten onmogelijk
 * maken via TypeScript" layer of the artui enforcement model.
 */

/**
 * Strings that look like alt text but communicate nothing to a screenreader.
 * Authors using these have not actually written alt text — they have written
 * a placeholder. The Image component rejects these at compile time.
 */
export type PlaceholderAltText =
  | 'image'
  | 'Image'
  | 'IMAGE'
  | 'img'
  | 'Img'
  | 'IMG'
  | 'photo'
  | 'Photo'
  | 'picture'
  | 'Picture'
  | 'icon'
  | 'Icon'
  | 'logo'
  | 'Logo'
  | ''
  | ' ';

/**
 * A non-empty, non-placeholder string. Used wherever an accessible name is
 * required (alt, aria-label, ...). Authors trying to pass `""` or `"image"`
 * get a type error.
 */
export type AccessibleText<T extends string = string> = T extends PlaceholderAltText
  ? never
  : T;

/**
 * Discriminated union for any interactive component that requires an
 * accessible name. Exactly one of these three sources MUST be present:
 *
 *  - visible text content (`children: string`)
 *  - `aria-label`
 *  - `aria-labelledby` (pointing at another element)
 *
 * Used by Button, IconButton, Modal close-button, and similar.
 */
export type AccessibleNameProps =
  | { children: AccessibleText; 'aria-label'?: never; 'aria-labelledby'?: never }
  | { children?: never; 'aria-label': AccessibleText; 'aria-labelledby'?: never }
  | { children?: never; 'aria-label'?: never; 'aria-labelledby': string };

components.name: Dialog
components.description: Accessible modal dialog using native <dialog> with showModal(). Browser-managed focus trap, top layer, and Escape handling. Follows WAI-ARIA APG Dialog Modal pattern.

props.name: title
props.description: Visible dialog heading rendered as an <h2>. Exactly one of title or aria-labelledby is required — passing neither or both is a compile error.

props.name: aria-labelledby
props.description: ID of an existing element that labels this dialog. Mutually exclusive with title.

props.name: open
props.description: Controls whether the dialog is shown.

props.name: onClose
props.description: Called when the user closes the dialog (close button, Escape, or backdrop click). The consumer is responsible for setting open to false.

props.name: children
props.description: Dialog body content. Rendering no content triggers a dev overlay.

props.name: description
props.description:
Optional supplementary description rendered as a

and wired to the dialog via aria-describedby.

props.name: initialFocusRef
props.description: Ref to the element that should receive focus when the dialog opens. Falls back to the first focusable descendant.

props.name: returnFocusRef
props.description: Ref to the element that should receive focus when the dialog closes. Falls back to the element that was focused before the dialog opened.

props.name: closeOnBackdropClick
props.description: Whether clicking the backdrop calls onClose.

props.name: className
props.description: Additional CSS class applied to the <dialog> element.

accessibility.description: The title prop renders a semantic <h2> inside the dialog. A dev overlay fires when children is empty so the developer sees the issue immediately.

The header close button is always rendered, so the dialog never has zero focus stops. A dev overlay fires when the dialog body has no focusable elements, prompting the developer to add interactive content.
Focus is trapped inside the dialog by the browser's native showModal() implementation. Escape always exits.
Focus moves to initialFocusRef (or the first focusable descendant) on open, and returns to the previously focused element (or returnFocusRef) on close.
The close button uses outline (not box-shadow) for its focus ring, preserving visibility in Windows High Contrast Mode.

The <dialog> element has role="dialog" natively, aria-modal="true", and aria-labelledby pointing at either the auto-generated title <h2> or a caller-supplied element. A dev overlay fires when aria-labelledby does not resolve to a DOM element.

examples.name: Basic with title
examples.description: Standard controlled dialog with a visible heading.
examples.code:
const [open, setOpen] = useState(false); setOpen(false)} title="Confirm deletion">

This action cannot be undone.

examples.name: With description
examples.description: Supplementary description wired via aria-describedby.
examples.code:
<Dialog
  open={open}
  onClose={() => setOpen(false)}
  title="Delete account"
  description="Your data will be permanently removed after 30 days."
>
  <button onClick={() => setOpen(false)}>Cancel</button>
  <button onClick={handleDelete}>Delete</button>
</Dialog>

examples.name: With initialFocusRef
examples.description: Focus lands on a specific element instead of the first focusable one.

examples.name: With aria-labelledby
examples.description: Label sourced from an external element.
examples.code:
Account settings

setOpen(false)} aria-labelledby="dialog-heading">

Manage your account details.

examples.name: No backdrop dismiss
examples.description: User must use the close button or Escape to exit.
examples.code:
setOpen(false)} title="Required confirmation" closeOnBackdropClick={false} >

Please confirm before continuing.

donts.reason: Missing label source. Compile error — every Dialog must have title or aria-labelledby so screen reader users know what the dialog is about.
Two label sources at once. Compile error — pass exactly one of title or aria-labelledby.
Empty children. A dev overlay fires with WCAG 1.3.1. Dialogs must contain content so screen reader users understand their purpose.

fileContents.content:
/**
 * Compile-time accessibility guarantees.
 *
 * These types make missing or placeholder accessibility data a TypeScript
 * error, not a silent runtime mistake. They are the §1.4.2 "fouten onmogelijk
 * maken via TypeScript" layer of the artui enforcement model.
 */

/**
 * Strings that look like alt text but communicate nothing to a screenreader.
 * Authors using these have not actually written alt text — they have written
 * a placeholder. The Image component rejects these at compile time.
 */
export type PlaceholderAltText =
  | 'image'
  | 'Image'
  | 'IMAGE'
  | 'img'
  | 'Img'
  | 'IMG'
  | 'photo'
  | 'Photo'
  | 'picture'
  | 'Picture'
  | 'icon'
  | 'Icon'
  | 'logo'
  | 'Logo'
  | ''
  | ' ';

/**
 * A non-empty, non-placeholder string. Used wherever an accessible name is
 * required (alt, aria-label, ...). Authors trying to pass `""` or `"image"`
 * get a type error.
 */
export type AccessibleText<T extends string = string> = T extends PlaceholderAltText
  ? never
  : T;

/**
 * Discriminated union for any interactive component that requires an
 * accessible name. Exactly one of these three sources MUST be present:
 *
 *  - visible text content (`children: string`)
 *  - `aria-label`
 *  - `aria-labelledby` (pointing at another element)
 *
 * Used by Button, IconButton, Modal close-button, and similar.
 */
export type AccessibleNameProps =
  | { children: AccessibleText; 'aria-label'?: never; 'aria-labelledby'?: never }
  | { children?: never; 'aria-label': AccessibleText; 'aria-labelledby'?: never }
  | { children?: never; 'aria-label'?: never; 'aria-labelledby': string };

components.name: DropdownMenu
components.description: Accessible dropdown menu following the WAI-ARIA APG Menu Button pattern. Supports single-level submenus, typeahead, full keyboard navigation, and compile-time enforcement of accessible names on triggers and items.

props.name: open
props.description: When provided, puts the root into controlled mode. Must be paired with onOpenChange.

props.name: onOpenChange
props.description: Called when the menu open state changes. Required when open is provided.

props.name: children
props.description: Must contain exactly one Trigger and one Content. Other sub-components go inside Content.

props.name: Trigger.children
props.description: Visible label text. Exactly one of children, aria-label, or aria-labelledby is required — compile error otherwise.

props.name: Trigger.aria-label
props.description: Invisible label for icon-only triggers. Mutually exclusive with children and aria-labelledby.

props.name: Trigger.aria-labelledby
props.description: ID of an external label element. Mutually exclusive with children and aria-label.

props.name: Trigger.disabled
props.description: Disables the trigger button.

props.name: Trigger.className
props.description: Additional CSS class applied to the trigger button.

props.name: Content.children
props.description: Menu items, separators, and Sub groups. Empty content triggers a dev overlay.

props.name: Content.className
props.description: Additional CSS class applied to the menu panel.

props.name: Item.onSelect
props.description: Called when the item is activated by click, Enter, or Space.

props.name: Item.disabled
props.description: Marks the item as aria-disabled and prevents selection.

props.name: Item.className
props.description: Additional CSS class applied to the item element.

props.name: Separator.className
props.description: Additional CSS class applied to the separator element.

props.name: SubTrigger.children
props.description: Visible label for the sub-trigger. One of children, aria-label, or aria-labelledby is required.

props.name: SubTrigger.aria-label
props.description: Invisible label for the sub-trigger.

props.name: SubTrigger.aria-labelledby
props.description: External label element ID for the sub-trigger.

props.name: SubTrigger.disabled
props.description: Disables the sub-trigger.

props.name: SubContent.children
props.description: Items inside the sub-menu.

props.name: SubContent.className
props.description: Additional CSS class applied to the sub-menu panel.

accessibility.description: The menu panel has role=menu; each item has role=menuitem; the separator has role=separator. A dev overlay fires when Content renders with no children.

Full APG keyboard contract: Arrow Down/Up to navigate items, Home/End for first/last, Enter/Space to select, Escape to close, typeahead to jump by first character. ArrowRight opens a sub-menu; ArrowLeft or Escape closes it.
Focus moves to the first menu item on open. On close via Escape, focus returns to the trigger. Sub-menus return focus to the sub-trigger on close.
Focus indicators use outline (not box-shadow) to remain visible in Windows High Contrast Mode.
The trigger button has a minimum 44×44 px touch target.

Trigger carries aria-haspopup=menu, aria-expanded, and aria-controls. The menu carries aria-labelledby pointing at the trigger. Sub-triggers carry the same trio for their sub-menus. Accessible names on Trigger and SubTrigger are enforced at compile time via AccessibleNameProps.

examples.name: Basic menu
examples.description: Uncontrolled dropdown with items and a separator.
examples.code:
<DropdownMenu>
  <DropdownMenu.Trigger>Account</DropdownMenu.Trigger>
  <DropdownMenu.Content>
    <DropdownMenu.Item onSelect={() => navigate('/profile')}>Profile</DropdownMenu.Item>
    <DropdownMenu.Item onSelect={() => navigate('/settings')}>Settings</DropdownMenu.Item>
    <DropdownMenu.Separator />
    <DropdownMenu.Item onSelect={signOut}>Sign out</DropdownMenu.Item>
  </DropdownMenu.Content>
</DropdownMenu>

examples.name: With submenu
examples.description: Single-level nested sub-menu.
examples.code:
<DropdownMenu>
  <DropdownMenu.Trigger>Account</DropdownMenu.Trigger>
  <DropdownMenu.Content>
    <DropdownMenu.Item onSelect={() => navigate('/profile')}>Profile</DropdownMenu.Item>
    <DropdownMenu.Sub>
      <DropdownMenu.SubTrigger>Preferences</DropdownMenu.SubTrigger>
      <DropdownMenu.SubContent>
        <DropdownMenu.Item onSelect={() => setTheme('light')}>Light theme</DropdownMenu.Item>
        <DropdownMenu.Item onSelect={() => setTheme('dark')}>Dark theme</DropdownMenu.Item>
      </DropdownMenu.SubContent>
    </DropdownMenu.Sub>
  </DropdownMenu.Content>
</DropdownMenu>

examples.name: Controlled
examples.description: Menu controlled by external state.
examples.code:
const [open, setOpen] = useState(false);

<DropdownMenu open={open} onOpenChange={setOpen}>
  <DropdownMenu.Trigger>Account</DropdownMenu.Trigger>
  <DropdownMenu.Content>
    <DropdownMenu.Item onSelect={() => navigate('/profile')}>Profile</DropdownMenu.Item>
  </DropdownMenu.Content>
</DropdownMenu>

examples.name: Icon-only trigger
examples.description: Trigger with no visible text — aria-label provides the accessible name.
examples.code:
<DropdownMenu>
  <DropdownMenu.Trigger aria-label="User menu">
    <UserIcon aria-hidden="true" />
  </DropdownMenu.Trigger>
  <DropdownMenu.Content>
    <DropdownMenu.Item onSelect={signOut}>Sign out</DropdownMenu.Item>
  </DropdownMenu.Content>
</DropdownMenu>

examples.name: Disabled items
examples.description: Individual items can be disabled without disabling the whole menu.
examples.code:
<DropdownMenu>
  <DropdownMenu.Trigger>Actions</DropdownMenu.Trigger>
  <DropdownMenu.Content>
    <DropdownMenu.Item onSelect={publish}>Publish</DropdownMenu.Item>
    <DropdownMenu.Item onSelect={archive} disabled>Archive (unavailable)</DropdownMenu.Item>
  </DropdownMenu.Content>
</DropdownMenu>

donts.reason: No accessible name. Compile error — every Trigger must have children, aria-label, or aria-labelledby so screen reader users know what the menu is for.
Empty string is not an accessible name. Compile error — the AccessibleText type rejects placeholder values.
Missing onSelect. Compile error — every Item must declare what happens when it is selected.
Empty content. A dev overlay fires with WCAG 1.3.1. A menu with no items gives keyboard and screen reader users nothing to interact with.

fileContents.content:
/**
 * Compile-time accessibility guarantees.
 *
 * These types make missing or placeholder accessibility data a TypeScript
 * error, not a silent runtime mistake. They are the §1.4.2 "fouten onmogelijk
 * maken via TypeScript" layer of the artui enforcement model.
 */

/**
 * Strings that look like alt text but communicate nothing to a screenreader.
 * Authors using these have not actually written alt text — they have written
 * a placeholder. The Image component rejects these at compile time.
 */
export type PlaceholderAltText =
  | 'image'
  | 'Image'
  | 'IMAGE'
  | 'img'
  | 'Img'
  | 'IMG'
  | 'photo'
  | 'Photo'
  | 'picture'
  | 'Picture'
  | 'icon'
  | 'Icon'
  | 'logo'
  | 'Logo'
  | ''
  | ' ';

/**
 * A non-empty, non-placeholder string. Used wherever an accessible name is
 * required (alt, aria-label, ...). Authors trying to pass `""` or `"image"`
 * get a type error.
 */
export type AccessibleText<T extends string = string> = T extends PlaceholderAltText
  ? never
  : T;

/**
 * Discriminated union for any interactive component that requires an
 * accessible name. Exactly one of these three sources MUST be present:
 *
 *  - visible text content (`children: string`)
 *  - `aria-label`
 *  - `aria-labelledby` (pointing at another element)
 *
 * Used by Button, IconButton, Modal close-button, and similar.
 */
export type AccessibleNameProps =
  | { children: AccessibleText; 'aria-label'?: never; 'aria-labelledby'?: never }
  | { children?: never; 'aria-label': AccessibleText; 'aria-labelledby'?: never }
  | { children?: never; 'aria-label'?: never; 'aria-labelledby': string };

components.name: Image
components.description: An wrapper that refuses to render without a meaningful alt or an explicit `decorative` flag.

props.name: alt
props.description: Description of what the image communicates. Required unless `decorative` is set. Rejects placeholder strings like "image" or "photo" at compile time.

props.name: decorative
props.description: Marks the image as purely decorative. Renders an empty alt and role="presentation". Mutually exclusive with `alt`.

accessibility.description: Every has either a meaningful text alternative or is explicitly marked decorative — the two valid choices under WCAG Non-text Content.
Decorative images are exposed with role="presentation" so assistive technology can skip them.

examples.name: Meaningful image
examples.description: Standard case — describe what the image shows.
examples.code: <Image src="/team.jpg" alt="The engineering team in the Ghent office, May 2026" />

examples.name: Decorative image
examples.description: Background photo that adds no information — opt out explicitly.
examples.code: <Image src="/hero-pattern.svg" decorative />

donts.reason: "image" tells a screenreader nothing the user couldn't already infer from the element role. Use real description text, or mark the image `decorative`.
An empty alt without `decorative` is ambiguous — was it left empty on purpose or forgotten? Pass `decorative` to make the intent explicit.

fileContents.content:
/**
 * Compile-time accessibility guarantees.
 *
 * These types make missing or placeholder accessibility data a TypeScript
 * error, not a silent runtime mistake. They are the §1.4.2 "fouten onmogelijk
 * maken via TypeScript" layer of the artui enforcement model.
 */

/**
 * Strings that look like alt text but communicate nothing to a screenreader.
 * Authors using these have not actually written alt text — they have written
 * a placeholder. The Image component rejects these at compile time.
 */
export type PlaceholderAltText =
  | 'image'
  | 'Image'
  | 'IMAGE'
  | 'img'
  | 'Img'
  | 'IMG'
  | 'photo'
  | 'Photo'
  | 'picture'
  | 'Picture'
  | 'icon'
  | 'Icon'
  | 'logo'
  | 'Logo'
  | ''
  | ' ';

/**
 * A non-empty, non-placeholder string. Used wherever an accessible name is
 * required (alt, aria-label, ...). Authors trying to pass `""` or `"image"`
 * get a type error.
 */
export type AccessibleText<T extends string = string> = T extends PlaceholderAltText
  ? never
  : T;

/**
 * Discriminated union for any interactive component that requires an
 * accessible name. Exactly one of these three sources MUST be present:
 *
 *  - visible text content (`children: string`)
 *  - `aria-label`
 *  - `aria-labelledby` (pointing at another element)
 *
 * Used by Button, IconButton, Modal close-button, and similar.
 */
export type AccessibleNameProps =
  | { children: AccessibleText; 'aria-label'?: never; 'aria-labelledby'?: never }
  | { children?: never; 'aria-label': AccessibleText; 'aria-labelledby'?: never }
  | { children?: never; 'aria-label'?: never; 'aria-labelledby': string };

components.name: Select
components.description: Accessible dual-mode select control built on native HTML elements. Single mode renders a real styled <select> — platform provides all a11y for free. Multi mode renders a hidden <select multiple> as the form/value source of truth, paired with a unified Control field: chips (inline), a disclosure trigger button, an optional clear-all button, and a decorative caret — all siblings inside one bordered container. Selection is communicated via removable chips and aria-live announcements. Full keyboard contract per APG Listbox, compile-time accessible-name enforcement via AccessibleNameProps, and dev-overlay runtime guards for empty content, empty labels, and duplicate values.

props.name: multiple
props.description: When true, switches to multi-select mode: renders a hidden <select multiple> and a custom ARIA listbox UI with Control, Content, and optional Group subcomponents. When absent or false, renders a plain native <select>.

props.name: value
props.description: Controlled value. A single string in single mode; an array of strings in multi mode. Pair with onValueChange.

props.name: defaultValue
props.description: Uncontrolled initial selection.

props.name: onValueChange
props.description: Called whenever the selection changes.

props.name: name
props.description: HTML name attribute forwarded to the native <select> (single) or hidden <select multiple> (multi) for form submission.

props.name: open
props.description: Multi mode only. When provided, puts the listbox into controlled-open mode. Pair with onOpenChange.

props.name: onOpenChange
props.description: Multi mode only. Called when the panel open state changes.

props.name: disabled
props.description: Disables the native select (single mode) or the trigger and all chip remove/clear-all buttons (multi mode).

props.name: aria-label
props.description: Single mode only. Accessible name for the native <select>. Exactly one of aria-label or aria-labelledby is required — compile error otherwise.

props.name: aria-labelledby
props.description: Single mode only. ID of an external element that labels the native <select>.

props.name: Control.aria-label
props.description: Accessible name for the trigger button inside the field. Exactly one of aria-label, aria-labelledby, or children is required — compile error otherwise.

props.name: Control.aria-labelledby
props.description: ID of an external element that labels the trigger button.

props.name: Control.children
props.description: Visible text label rendered inside the trigger button.

props.name: Control.placeholder
props.description: Text shown inside the trigger button when nothing is selected. Hidden when chips are present.

props.name: Control.removeLabel
props.description: Customise the remove button's accessible name per chip. Defaults to `Remove ${label}`.

props.name: Control.showClearAll
props.description: When true, renders a clear-all button at the right of the field (left of the caret). Only visible when at least one option is selected.

props.name: Control.clearAllLabel
props.description: Accessible name for the clear-all button.

props.name: Control.className
props.description: Additional CSS class on the control field container.

props.name: Content.children
props.description: Select.Option and Select.Group elements. An empty Content triggers a dev overlay (WCAG 1.3.1).

props.name: Content.className
props.description: Additional CSS class on the listbox panel.

props.name: Option.value
props.description: Unique identifier for this option. Duplicate values trigger a dev overlay (WCAG 4.1.2). In single mode maps to a native <option>; in multi mode maps to both a native <option> and an ARIA option div.

props.name: Option.children
props.description: Visible label. Empty or whitespace-only children trigger a dev overlay (WCAG 1.3.1).

props.name: Option.disabled
props.description: Marks the option as disabled. In single mode sets the native disabled attribute. In multi mode sets aria-disabled and excludes the option from keyboard navigation.

props.name: Option.className
props.description: Additional CSS class on the option element (multi mode custom panel only).

props.name: Group.label
props.description: Group heading. In single mode maps to <optgroup label>; in multi mode becomes role=group aria-label and a visible (aria-hidden) header. Empty label triggers a dev overlay (WCAG 1.3.1).

props.name: Group.disabled
props.description: Disables the entire group. In single mode sets the disabled attribute on <optgroup>; in multi mode individual Option children must also be marked disabled for keyboard exclusion.

props.name: Group.children
props.description: Select.Option elements inside this group.

props.name: Group.className
props.description: Additional CSS class on the group container (multi mode custom panel only).

accessibility.description: Selected options are communicated outside the listbox via removable chips inline in the Control field; each chip's remove button has an accessible name derived from the option label (default: 'Remove <label>', customisable via removeLabel). A visually-hidden aria-live=polite region announces every selection change ('Belgium added, 3 selected') and clear-all ('All selections cleared'). Dev overlays fire for: empty Content, empty/whitespace Option labels, empty Group labels. Option groups are conveyed semantically as role=group with aria-label in the custom panel and <optgroup label> in the hidden native select.

Single mode: keyboard fully delegated to the native <select>. Multi mode: Arrow Down/Up move focus between options (no wrap, APG Listbox); Home/End jump to first/last; Space or Enter toggle selection without closing; Escape closes and returns focus to trigger; Tab closes naturally; printable-character typeahead jumps to first match; Backspace on the trigger removes the last chip. All selection changes route through onValueChange (onChange-driven, registry masked-input rule).

Focus moves to the first focusable option when the multi-mode panel opens. Escape and Tab return focus to the trigger. Clear-all moves focus to the trigger after clearing. When the last chip is removed (via remove button or Backspace), focus is rescued to the trigger.

Focus indicators: the Control field shows a :focus-within outline scoped to :has(.artui-select-trigger:focus-visible), so only keyboard focus (not mouse clicks) triggers the ring. Individual chip remove buttons and the clear-all button have :focus-visible outlines. All indicators are visible in Windows High Contrast Mode via the forced-colors @media block.

All interactive elements have a minimum 44px touch target: native select (min-height), Control field (min-height 44px), chip remove buttons (padding), clear-all button (padding). The trigger fills the remaining field width, enlarging the click area proportionally.
Toggling an option in multi mode never causes an unexpected context change — onValueChange is a local state update only and the panel stays open.

Single mode: the native <select> carries role, name, and value automatically. Accessible name enforced at compile time. Multi mode: the trigger inside Control carries aria-haspopup=listbox, aria-expanded, aria-controls; accessible name enforced at compile time via AccessibleNameProps on Control. Custom panel carries role=listbox, aria-multiselectable=true, aria-labelledby. Each custom Option carries role=option and explicit aria-selected (true or false, never omitted). Hidden <select multiple> stays in sync for form submission. The Control container is a presentational div — no interactive nesting violations. Dev overlays fire for duplicate option values and empty content.
A visually-hidden role=status region with aria-live=polite and aria-atomic=true announces all selection changes and clear-all to screen readers.
CSS uses system color tokens (Canvas, ButtonFace, Highlight, ButtonText) in the forced-colors @media block, preserving 3:1 non-text contrast in Windows High Contrast Mode.

examples.name: Single mode
examples.description: Native <select> with accessible name and option groups.
examples.code:
<label id="country-label">Country</label>
<Select aria-labelledby="country-label" name="country" onValueChange={setCountry}>
  <Select.Group label="Benelux">
    <Select.Option value="be">Belgium</Select.Option>
    <Select.Option value="nl">Netherlands</Select.Option>
  </Select.Group>
  <Select.Option value="fr">France</Select.Option>
</Select>

examples.name: Multi mode — basic uncontrolled
examples.description: Unified control field with removable chips and a custom listbox.
examples.code:
<Select multiple defaultValue={["be"]} onValueChange={setCountries}>
  <Select.Control aria-label="Countries" />
  <Select.Content>
    <Select.Option value="be">Belgium</Select.Option>
    <Select.Option value="nl">Netherlands</Select.Option>
    <Select.Option value="fr" disabled>France</Select.Option>
  </Select.Content>
</Select>

examples.name: Multi mode — with groups
examples.description: Options organised into accessible groups.
examples.code:
<Select multiple defaultValue={["be"]} onValueChange={setCountries}>
  <Select.Control aria-label="Countries" />
  <Select.Content>
    <Select.Group label="Benelux">
      <Select.Option value="be">Belgium</Select.Option>
      <Select.Option value="nl">Netherlands</Select.Option>
      <Select.Option value="lu">Luxembourg</Select.Option>
    </Select.Group>
    <Select.Group label="Other">
      <Select.Option value="fr">France</Select.Option>
      <Select.Option value="de">Germany</Select.Option>
    </Select.Group>
  </Select.Content>
</Select>

examples.name: Multi mode — with clear-all
examples.description: Clear-all button lets users remove all chips at once.
examples.code:
<Select multiple defaultValue={["be", "nl"]} onValueChange={setCountries}>
  <Select.Control aria-label="Countries" showClearAll clearAllLabel="Clear all countries" />
  <Select.Content>
    <Select.Option value="be">Belgium</Select.Option>
    <Select.Option value="nl">Netherlands</Select.Option>
    <Select.Option value="fr">France</Select.Option>
  </Select.Content>
</Select>

examples.name: Multi mode — controlled
examples.description: External state manages the selection.
examples.code:
const [countries, setCountries] = useState<readonly string[]>(['be']);

<Select multiple value={countries} onValueChange={setCountries}>
  <Select.Control aria-label="Countries" />
  <Select.Content>
    <Select.Option value="be">Belgium</Select.Option>
    <Select.Option value="nl">Netherlands</Select.Option>
  </Select.Content>
</Select>

examples.name: Multi mode — custom remove label (i18n)
examples.description: Override the remove-button accessible name for localisation.
examples.code:
<Select multiple defaultValue={["be"]} onValueChange={setCountries}>
  <Select.Control
    aria-label="Pays"
    removeLabel={(label) => `Supprimer ${label}`}
  />
  <Select.Content>
    <Select.Option value="be">Belgique</Select.Option>
  </Select.Content>
</Select>

donts.reason: Single mode with no accessible name. Compile error — aria-label or aria-labelledby is required so screen reader users know what the control is for.
No accessible name on Control. Compile error — every multi-mode Control must have aria-label, aria-labelledby, or children.
Empty Content. A dev overlay fires with WCAG 1.3.1. A listbox with no options gives keyboard and screen reader users nothing to interact with.
Whitespace-only label. A dev overlay fires with WCAG 1.3.1. Screen readers announce nothing useful for this option.
Duplicate value. A dev guard logs with WCAG 4.1.2. Duplicate values make selection state ambiguous for both the UI and assistive technology.
Empty group label. A dev overlay fires with WCAG 1.3.1. Screen readers cannot identify the group.

fileContents.content:
/**
 * Compile-time accessibility guarantees.
 *
 * These types make missing or placeholder accessibility data a TypeScript
 * error, not a silent runtime mistake. They are the §1.4.2 "fouten onmogelijk
 * maken via TypeScript" layer of the artui enforcement model.
 */

/**
 * Strings that look like alt text but communicate nothing to a screenreader.
 * Authors using these have not actually written alt text — they have written
 * a placeholder. The Image component rejects these at compile time.
 */
export type PlaceholderAltText =
  | 'image'
  | 'Image'
  | 'IMAGE'
  | 'img'
  | 'Img'
  | 'IMG'
  | 'photo'
  | 'Photo'
  | 'picture'
  | 'Picture'
  | 'icon'
  | 'Icon'
  | 'logo'
  | 'Logo'
  | ''
  | ' ';

/**
 * A non-empty, non-placeholder string. Used wherever an accessible name is
 * required (alt, aria-label, ...). Authors trying to pass `""` or `"image"`
 * get a type error.
 */
export type AccessibleText<T extends string = string> = T extends PlaceholderAltText
  ? never
  : T;

/**
 * Discriminated union for any interactive component that requires an
 * accessible name. Exactly one of these three sources MUST be present:
 *
 *  - visible text content (`children: string`)
 *  - `aria-label`
 *  - `aria-labelledby` (pointing at another element)
 *
 * Used by Button, IconButton, Modal close-button, and similar.
 */
export type AccessibleNameProps =
  | { children: AccessibleText; 'aria-label'?: never; 'aria-labelledby'?: never }
  | { children?: never; 'aria-label': AccessibleText; 'aria-labelledby'?: never }
  | { children?: never; 'aria-label'?: never; 'aria-labelledby': string };

components.name: Slider
components.description: Accessible slider for single-thumb and two-thumb range selection. Follows WAI-ARIA APG Slider and Slider (Multi-Thumb) patterns. Enforces per-thumb accessible names at compile time, wires aria-valuetext automatically from a format function, and keeps dependent aria-valuemin/aria-valuemax in sync when thumbs approach each other.

props.name: min
props.description: Minimum value.

props.name: max
props.description: Maximum value.

props.name: step
props.description: Arrow-key step size. A dev overlay fires when step ≤ 0 or step > (max - min).

props.name: largeStep
props.description: PageUp / PageDown jump size.

props.name: disabled
props.description: Disables interaction. Uses aria-disabled rather than the HTML disabled attribute so the thumb stays in the tab order for AT inspection.

props.name: orientation
props.description: Slider orientation.

props.name: formatValue
props.description: When provided, the returned string is wired to aria-valuetext on every thumb automatically. Use for non-numeric domains (e.g. '€250', 'Monday').

props.name: value
props.description: Controlled value. Pair with onValueChange.

props.name: defaultValue
props.description: Uncontrolled initial value.

props.name: onValueChange
props.description: Called whenever the value changes.

props.name: thumbs
props.description: Range mode: exactly two thumb descriptors. Each must satisfy AccessibleNameProps — passing neither children, aria-label, nor aria-labelledby is a compile error.

props.name: aria-label
props.description: Single-thumb: accessible name on the thumb button. Range: accessible name on the group wrapper. One of aria-label or aria-labelledby is required in range mode.

props.name: aria-labelledby
props.description: Single-thumb: ID of an element that labels the thumb. Range: ID of the element that labels the group. A dev overlay fires when the ID does not resolve to a DOM element.

props.name: className
props.description: Additional CSS class applied to the slider root element.

accessibility.description: Single-thumb sliders inherit their accessible name from AccessibleNameProps (compile-time required). Range sliders wrap in role="group" with aria-label or aria-labelledby; a dev overlay fires when neither is provided or when aria-labelledby points to a missing DOM element.

ArrowLeft/Right/Up/Down move the focused thumb by step. PageUp/PageDown move by largeStep (defaults to max(step*10, (max-min)/10) per APG). Home/End jump to the effective min/max. A dev overlay fires when step is invalid.
Each thumb is a native <button> in the natural tab order. No focus trap is installed; Tab and Shift+Tab always leave the slider.
Track-fill and thumb position transitions are gated on @media (prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference). With reduced motion the slider snaps without animation.
Tab order is constant regardless of visual thumb position. Thumb buttons are always rendered in the same DOM order (thumbs[0] first, thumbs[1] second); visual overlap is handled by z-index only.
Focus indicators use outline (not box-shadow) so they are visible in Windows High Contrast Mode.
Thumb buttons have a 44×44 CSS px hit area via a ::before pseudo-element, satisfying the recommended target size. The visual disc is smaller for aesthetics.
Adjusting the value never causes a context change — onValueChange is a local state update only.

Each thumb has role="slider", aria-valuemin, aria-valuemax, aria-valuenow, aria-orientation, and its accessible name. aria-valuetext is wired automatically when formatValue is supplied. Range mode: per-thumb names enforced at compile time via the readonly [SliderThumbDescriptor, SliderThumbDescriptor] tuple. When a thumb's value crosses the other thumb's value (via keyboard or pointer drag), focus and value-identity transfer to the other thumb so the gesture continues seamlessly. Each thumb's aria-valuemin and aria-valuemax recompute on every render based on the current value ordering. Dev overlays fire for missing thumb names, out-of-range values, and type mismatches.
Bundled CSS uses system color tokens (Canvas, CanvasText, currentColor) that maintain 3:1 non-text contrast. Windows High Contrast Mode overrides apply ButtonFace/ButtonText/Highlight tokens.

examples.name: Single-thumb price slider
examples.description: Uncontrolled with aria-valuetext wired from formatValue.
examples.code:
<Slider
  min={0}
  max={1000}
  step={10}
  defaultValue={250}
  aria-label="Maximum price"
  formatValue={(v) => `€${v}`}
/>

examples.name: Range slider
examples.description: Two-thumb range with per-thumb names enforced at compile time.
examples.code:
<Slider
  min={0}
  max={1000}
  step={10}
  defaultValue={[100, 750]}
  aria-label="Price range"
  formatValue={(v) => `€${v}`}
  thumbs={[
    { 'aria-label': 'Minimum price' },
    { 'aria-label': 'Maximum price' },
  ]}
/>

examples.name: Non-numeric domain
examples.description: Weekday picker using formatValue for user-friendly value text.

examples.name: Vertical orientation
examples.description: Volume slider oriented vertically.
examples.code:
<Slider
  min={0}
  max={100}
  step={1}
  defaultValue={50}
  orientation="vertical"
  aria-label="Volume"
/>

examples.name: Controlled
examples.description: External state manages the value.

donts.reason: Empty thumb descriptors. Compile error — each SliderThumbDescriptor must satisfy AccessibleNameProps (aria-label, aria-labelledby, or children required).
Range slider without a group label. A dev overlay fires with WCAG 1.3.1 [Slider:range-without-group-name]. Add aria-label or aria-labelledby on the Slider root.
defaultValue outside [min, max]. A dev overlay fires with WCAG 4.1.2 [Slider:value-out-of-range] and the value is clamped silently.

fileContents.content:
/**
 * Compile-time accessibility guarantees.
 *
 * These types make missing or placeholder accessibility data a TypeScript
 * error, not a silent runtime mistake. They are the §1.4.2 "fouten onmogelijk
 * maken via TypeScript" layer of the artui enforcement model.
 */

/**
 * Strings that look like alt text but communicate nothing to a screenreader.
 * Authors using these have not actually written alt text — they have written
 * a placeholder. The Image component rejects these at compile time.
 */
export type PlaceholderAltText =
  | 'image'
  | 'Image'
  | 'IMAGE'
  | 'img'
  | 'Img'
  | 'IMG'
  | 'photo'
  | 'Photo'
  | 'picture'
  | 'Picture'
  | 'icon'
  | 'Icon'
  | 'logo'
  | 'Logo'
  | ''
  | ' ';

/**
 * A non-empty, non-placeholder string. Used wherever an accessible name is
 * required (alt, aria-label, ...). Authors trying to pass `""` or `"image"`
 * get a type error.
 */
export type AccessibleText<T extends string = string> = T extends PlaceholderAltText
  ? never
  : T;

/**
 * Discriminated union for any interactive component that requires an
 * accessible name. Exactly one of these three sources MUST be present:
 *
 *  - visible text content (`children: string`)
 *  - `aria-label`
 *  - `aria-labelledby` (pointing at another element)
 *
 * Used by Button, IconButton, Modal close-button, and similar.
 */
export type AccessibleNameProps =
  | { children: AccessibleText; 'aria-label'?: never; 'aria-labelledby'?: never }
  | { children?: never; 'aria-label': AccessibleText; 'aria-labelledby'?: never }
  | { children?: never; 'aria-label'?: never; 'aria-labelledby': string };

components.name: Toast
components.description: Accessible toast notification system following WAI-ARIA live-region patterns. Supports info/success/warning/error types, auto-dismiss with pause-on-hover, keyboard focus management (Alt+T shortcut), and compile-time enforcement of accessible notification titles.

props.name: children
props.description: Application content. Mount exactly one ToastProvider at the root of your app.

props.name: maxVisible
props.description: Maximum number of toasts visible at once per region. Oldest is collapsed when the limit is exceeded.

props.name: defaultDuration
props.description: Default auto-dismiss duration in milliseconds. Pass null for persistent toasts that require explicit dismissal.

props.name: ToastOptions.title
props.description: Required accessible title for the notification. Empty strings and placeholder values are rejected at compile time.

props.name: ToastOptions.description
props.description: Optional secondary body text shown below the title.

props.name: ToastOptions.type
props.description: Notification severity. info and success are polite; warning and error are assertive.

props.name: ToastOptions.duration
props.description: Per-toast duration override. null makes the toast persistent. Action toasts have a 10 s minimum.

props.name: ToastOptions.action
props.description: Optional action button. Rendered as a real <button>; invoking it dismisses the toast.

props.name: ToastAction.label
props.description: Visible label for the action button. Placeholder values are compile errors.

props.name: ToastAction.onAction
props.description: Called when the action button is activated.

accessibility.description: Info and success toasts are rendered in an aria-live=polite region (role=status). Warning and error toasts are rendered in an aria-live=assertive region (role=alert). Both regions are mounted as persistent DOM nodes so screen readers maintain their subscription even after all toasts dismiss.
Timers pause on hover, keyboard focus, document hidden, and prefers-reduced-motion. The 10 s minimum is enforced for action toasts. A dev warning fires for error toasts with short durations.
Esc dismisses the focused toast. Alt+T moves focus to the most-recent toast's action or close button. Action and close are real <button> elements activatable by keyboard.

Toasts do not steal focus on render. Focus returns to the element that was focused when show() was called, when the toast is dismissed via close/action/Esc — unless the user has already moved focus elsewhere.
Type (info/success/warning/error) is conveyed via a visually-hidden text prefix on the title, not color alone.
Focus indicators on action and close buttons use outline (not box-shadow) to remain visible in Windows High Contrast Mode.

examples.name: Basic
examples.description: Show a simple informational toast.
examples.code:
const toast = useToast();
toast.info("Profile saved.");

examples.name: Error with action
examples.description: Persistent error toast with an undo action.

examples.name: Custom duration
examples.description: Override the default auto-dismiss duration.
examples.code: toast.show({ title: "Copied!", type: "success", duration: 2000 });

donts.reason: Empty title is not an accessible name. Compile error — AccessibleText rejects empty strings.
Error toasts with short durations and no action fail WCAG 2.2.1. A dev warning fires and duration should be null or ≥ 10 s.

fileContents.content:
/**
 * Compile-time accessibility guarantees.
 *
 * These types make missing or placeholder accessibility data a TypeScript
 * error, not a silent runtime mistake. They are the §1.4.2 "fouten onmogelijk
 * maken via TypeScript" layer of the artui enforcement model.
 */

/**
 * Strings that look like alt text but communicate nothing to a screenreader.
 * Authors using these have not actually written alt text — they have written
 * a placeholder. The Image component rejects these at compile time.
 */
export type PlaceholderAltText =
  | 'image'
  | 'Image'
  | 'IMAGE'
  | 'img'
  | 'Img'
  | 'IMG'
  | 'photo'
  | 'Photo'
  | 'picture'
  | 'Picture'
  | 'icon'
  | 'Icon'
  | 'logo'
  | 'Logo'
  | ''
  | ' ';

/**
 * A non-empty, non-placeholder string. Used wherever an accessible name is
 * required (alt, aria-label, ...). Authors trying to pass `""` or `"image"`
 * get a type error.
 */
export type AccessibleText<T extends string = string> = T extends PlaceholderAltText
  ? never
  : T;

/**
 * Discriminated union for any interactive component that requires an
 * accessible name. Exactly one of these three sources MUST be present:
 *
 *  - visible text content (`children: string`)
 *  - `aria-label`
 *  - `aria-labelledby` (pointing at another element)
 *
 * Used by Button, IconButton, Modal close-button, and similar.
 */
export type AccessibleNameProps =
  | { children: AccessibleText; 'aria-label'?: never; 'aria-labelledby'?: never }
  | { children?: never; 'aria-label': AccessibleText; 'aria-labelledby'?: never }
  | { children?: never; 'aria-label'?: never; 'aria-labelledby': string };
